17   Consider the following functions deﬁned for x > 1 :
                                                    √  
                                     f(x) = log2 log2 x

                                                                 
                                      g(x) = log2        log2 x


     Which one of the following is true for all values of x > 1 ?

     A 0 ≤ f(x) ≤ g(x) or g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ 0

     B 0 ≤ g(x) ≤ f(x) or f(x) ≤ g(x) ≤ 0
         1
     C   2
           ≤ f(x) ≤ g(x)   or g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ 12
         1
     D   2
           ≤ g(x) ≤ f(x)   or f(x) ≤ g(x) ≤ 12

     E 1 ≤ f(x) ≤ g(x) or g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ 1

     F 1 ≤ g(x) ≤ f(x) or f(x) ≤ g(x) ≤ 1




                                             19

stem.rawText:
Consider the following functions deﬁned for x > 1 :
                                                D    √ E
                                     f(x) = log2 log2 x

                                                    DB            E
                                      g(x) = log2        log2 x


     Which one of the following is true for all values of x > 1 ?

solution.rawText:
The expressions for f(x) and g(x) look quite complicated, so we start by simplifying them using
the laws of logarithms:
                                                    √ 
                                    f(x) = log2 log2 x
                                          = log2 ( 21 log2 x)
                                          = −1 + log2 (log2 x)
                                                 p        
                                     g(x) = log2   log2 x
                                          = 12 log2 (log2 x)

We now see that both f(x) and g(x) can be written as a function of log2 (log2 x). As x > 1,
log2 x > 0 and log2 (log2 x) can take any real value.
Let us write z = log2 (log2 x), so that we have f(x) = z − 1 and g(x) = 21 z. We can sketch a
graph showing these two functions of z:




                                                         f(x) = z − 1



                                             1                      g(x) = 21 z


                                                 0              2           z




We can calculate the intersection of y = z − 1 and y = 12 z, obtaining z = 2, y = 1.
We can then see from the graph that f(x) ≥ g(x) when both are at least 1, and f(x) ≤ g(x) when
both are at most 1, so either f(x) ≥ g(x) ≥ 1 or f(x) ≤ g(x) ≤ 1 (or both), which is option F.
(Note that the ‘or’ in the options is inclusive ‘or’, so we are allowed to have both of the inequal-
ities being true.)
